03 The Claude API — Your First AI Script

← Back to lessons

Hands on keyboard. You've learned the concepts. Now you call the API, get a response, and stream it to your terminal.

Mental Model: The Interview

An API call to Claude is like conducting an interview. You set the context (system prompt), ask questions (user messages), and get responses (assistant messages). A conversation is just a series of these exchanges, each one building on the last.

Key difference from a chat UI: When you use Claude in a browser, the interface manages the conversation for you. With the API, you manage it. You decide what context to include, what to remember, what to forget. Total control.

Setup

# Install the Anthropic SDK
npm install @anthropic-ai/sdk

# Set your API key (get one at console.anthropic.com)
export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="sk-ant-..."

Never commit your API key. Use environment variables or a .env file (with .env in your .gitignore). Leaked API keys get scraped by bots within minutes.

Your First API Call

import Anthropic from "@anthropic-ai/sdk";

const client = new Anthropic();

const response = await client.messages.create({
  model: "claude-sonnet-4-20250514",
  max_tokens: 1024,
  system: "You are a fact-checking assistant for journalists. Be concise and cite sources.",
  messages: [
    {
      role: "user",
      content: "Is it true that the US national debt first exceeded $30 trillion in 2022?"
    }
  ]
});

console.log(response.content[0].text);

Let's break down every parameter:

ParameterWhat it does
modelWhich Claude model to use. Sonnet is the sweet spot of speed + quality + cost.
max_tokensMaximum tokens in the response. Safety limit — the model stops here even if it's mid-sentence.
systemThe system prompt. Sets behavior for the entire conversation.
messagesArray of conversation turns. Each has a role ("user" or "assistant") and content.

The response object: response.content is an array of content blocks. For text responses, it's [{ type: "text", text: "..." }]. For tool use (Lesson 04), it can contain tool call blocks too. Always access response.content[0].text for simple text responses.

Multi-Turn Conversations

To have a conversation, you include the full message history with each call. The model has no memory — you provide the context every time.

const messages = [
  { role: "user", content: "Who is the current governor of Texas?" },
  { role: "assistant", content: "As of my last update, Greg Abbott is the governor of Texas." },
  { role: "user", content: "When did he take office?" }
];

const response = await client.messages.create({
  model: "claude-sonnet-4-20250514",
  max_tokens: 512,
  system: "You are a political research assistant. Be precise about dates and sourcing.",
  messages
});

// The model sees the full conversation and can reference earlier turns

This is the key insight: Each API call is stateless. The conversation "memory" is just the messages array you send. This means you can edit history, remove messages, summarize old context, or restart at any point. You're the editor, not the model.

Streaming: Token by Token

Without streaming, you wait for the entire response before seeing anything. With streaming, tokens appear as they're generated — like watching someone type.

const stream = client.messages.stream({
  model: "claude-sonnet-4-20250514",
  max_tokens: 1024,
  system: "You are a newsroom assistant.",
  messages: [
    { role: "user", content: "Write a 3-paragraph summary of the key issues in local journalism funding." }
  ]
});

// Print each chunk as it arrives
stream.on("text", (text) => {
  process.stdout.write(text);
});

// Get the final complete message
const finalMessage = await stream.finalMessage();
console.log("\n\nTotal tokens:", finalMessage.usage.input_tokens + finalMessage.usage.output_tokens);

Streaming is essential for any user-facing application. Nobody wants to stare at a spinner for 5 seconds. We'll build full streaming UIs in Lesson 09.

Important Optional Parameters

const response = await client.messages.create({
  model: "claude-sonnet-4-20250514",
  max_tokens: 2048,
  system: "You are a research assistant.",
  messages: [{ role: "user", content: "..." }],

  // Temperature: 0 = deterministic, 1 = creative
  temperature: 0,

  // Stop sequences: model stops when it generates any of these
  stop_sequences: ["\n\nHuman:", "END_OF_ANALYSIS"],

  // Metadata: tag requests for tracking/billing
  metadata: { user_id: "trevor-research-001" }
});
Use temperature: 0
// Fact extraction
// Classification
// Data parsing
// Structured output
// Code generation
Use temperature: 0.7-1.0
// Headline brainstorming
// Creative writing
// Generating alternatives
// Exploring angles
// Story ideation

Error Handling

import Anthropic from "@anthropic-ai/sdk";

const client = new Anthropic();

try {
  const response = await client.messages.create({
    model: "claude-sonnet-4-20250514",
    max_tokens: 1024,
    messages: [{ role: "user", content: "Hello" }]
  });
  console.log(response.content[0].text);
} catch (error) {
  if (error instanceof Anthropic.RateLimitError) {
    console.error("Rate limited. Wait and retry.");
  } else if (error instanceof Anthropic.AuthenticationError) {
    console.error("Bad API key. Check ANTHROPIC_API_KEY.");
  } else if (error instanceof Anthropic.APIError) {
    console.error(`API error ${error.status}: ${error.message}`);
  } else {
    throw error;
  }
}

Rate limits: The API has rate limits on requests per minute and tokens per minute. For production apps, implement exponential backoff. The SDK has built-in retry logic for transient errors, but you should handle persistent errors gracefully.

Cost Awareness

Every API call has a cost. The response includes token usage:

console.log(response.usage);
// { input_tokens: 142, output_tokens: 387 }

// Claude Sonnet pricing (as of 2025):
// Input:  $3 / million tokens
// Output: $15 / million tokens
// This call: (142 * 3 + 387 * 15) / 1_000_000 = $0.006
// About half a cent.

Journalism context: Analyzing 100 articles with a 500-token prompt and 300-token response each would cost roughly $0.60. Running a RAG pipeline over your entire archive of 10,000 articles might cost $50–100 for the initial embedding. Know your costs before you scale.

Check Your Understanding

1. How does the API "remember" conversation history?

It doesn't. You send the full message history with every API call. The messages array IS the memory. The model is stateless.

2. What's the difference between client.messages.create() and client.messages.stream()?

create() waits for the complete response before returning. stream() returns tokens incrementally as they're generated. Use streaming for any user-facing application to reduce perceived latency.

3. Why is max_tokens important?

It's a safety limit on response length. Without it, a model could generate an extremely long (and expensive) response. It also prevents runaway costs. Set it to the maximum length you'd reasonably expect for the task.

4. Where should you store your API key?

In environment variables or a .env file that's in your .gitignore. Never hardcode it in source files, never commit it to git. The SDK automatically reads ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from the environment.

Exercise: Build a CLI Interview Tool

Build a Node.js script that:

  1. Takes a topic as a command-line argument: node interview.js "local journalism funding"
  2. Has a system prompt that makes Claude act as a research assistant for investigative journalists
  3. Streams the response to the terminal
  4. After the response, prints the token count and estimated cost

Bonus: Add multi-turn conversation support using readline so you can ask follow-up questions.
